refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent verification and clearer traceability to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable to input parameters and stakeholder requirements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission inputs.
FR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance targets in a retrievable format.
FR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance target baselines and calibration updates.
FR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall establish traceability between worksite inputs and derived performance targets.

candidate_dps: DP1: Capture worksite mission context (operational objectives, environmental constraints, safety policies) via structured intake process; parse into a normalized context model; apply context-to-performance mapping rules to derive candidate performance targets; validate targets against worksite constraints; document traceability chain from context inputs to each target.

DP2: Maintain a parameterized performance baseline repository indexed by worksite context signature; upon new mission input, query repository for similar historical baselines; apply delta-adjustment rules based on context differences; present adjusted targets as candidates; require explicit acceptance or override by authorized operator.

DP3: Decompose worksite mission into functional sub-goals (safety, productivity, resource efficiency); assign performance dimensions (speed, accuracy, reliability, cost) to each sub-goal; synthesize cross-dimensional trade-off matrix; compute Pareto-optimal target set; present ranked candidates with trade-off justifications.

DP4: Implement a rule-based inference engine that accepts worksite context facts (terrain type, crew size, equipment inventory, safety tier) as inputs; fire context-to-requirement rules to generate performance targets; maintain audit trail of rule firings; support manual override with justification logging.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Capture worksite mission context via structured intake; normalize into context model; apply context-to-performance mapping rules; validate against constraints; document full traceability from context inputs to performance targets.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between context intake format and mapping rule engine—mitigated by defining a schema-based context model that decouples intake UI from rule logic.
Dependency on worksite safety plan and operational policies as external inputs—acceptable as these are given constraints, not system-owned; interface must be clearly specified.

Traceability documentation could become unwieldy if not structured; recommend adopting a formal traceability matrix format (e.g., context input ID → mapping rule ID → target ID) to avoid ad-hoc coupling.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low coupling because it separates concerns: (1) context capture (intake), (2) normalization (schema), (3) mapping logic (rules), and (4) validation (constraint checker). Each can be evolved independently. DP2 introduces historical dependency that may obscure first-principles reasoning. DP3 adds complexity (trade-off matrix) not required by FR. DP4 is functionally equivalent to DP1 but uses 'inference engine' terminology that may over-specify implementation.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context can be captured in a finite, repeatable set of structured fields (e.g., terrain, crew size, equipment list, safety tier). RISK: Unstructured or novel context may not fit schema; mitigation: design intake with extensible custom-field option and escalation path.

ASSUMPTION: A deterministic mapping from context to performance targets exists and is maintainable. RISK: Mapping rules may become inconsistent or outdated as operational practices evolve. MITIGATION: version rules, require periodic review, maintain change log.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable inputs available at intake time. RISK: Policies may change mid-mission or be ambiguous. MITIGATION: require explicit policy version/date in context input; flag conflicts during validation.

ASSUMPTION: 'Documented set of performance targets' implies a single authoritative output per mission context. RISK: Multiple valid target sets may exist; unclear which to select. MITIGATION: DP1 should produce ranked candidates with justification, not a single target.
ASSUMPTION: Traceability history can be maintained without significant overhead. RISK: Audit trail may grow large; queries slow. MITIGATION: define retention policy and indexing strategy upfront.

ASSUMPTION: Acceptance criteria 'maintains a history of baselines/calibration updates' implies versioning is in scope. RISK: Version control logic not addressed in DP1. MITIGATION: explicitly add version management as a sub-function within DP1 or as a separate FR.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly separate two concerns: (1) FR-A: 'Derive initial performance targets from worksite mission context' (addressed by DP1), and (2) FR-B: 'Maintain and manage a versioned history of performance baselines and calibration updates' (requires separate DP focused on baseline lifecycle: capture, store, retrieve, update, audit). This decomposition avoids conflating derivation logic with version control, reducing coupling and improving testability. Additionally, clarify acceptance criterion: does 'documented set' mean a single canonical target or a ranked set of candidates? If the latter, specify ranking criteria (e.g., safety-first, cost-optimized, balanced). Finally, define the interface contract between NGHE and external worksite safety/policy systems: what format, frequency, and validation rules apply to policy inputs?

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Mission context intake, normalization, context-to-performance mapping, constraint validation, and traceability documentation for worksite operations

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission context inputs (worksite parameters, objectives, constraints) from external tasking authority

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs normalized context model, performance targets, and traceability records for downstream consumption by Vehicle and Teleoperation

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints during intake; outputs validated safety-performance mappings and constraint violations

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Mission context intake, normalization, and mapping rules are core NGHE responsibilities. Constraint validation and traceability documentation align with solution-level orchestration. Minor diffusion: performance targets must flow to Vehicle/Teleoperation via Telemetry interface.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/operational platform, not a mission planning or context normalization layer. Context intake and mapping rules would be misplaced in vehicle-level logic. High diffusion: mission context logic would need to be replicated or accessed remotely.

Weak fit. Teleoperation handles operator-vehicle interaction and real-time control, not mission context modeling or constraint mapping. Context normalization belongs upstream. High diffusion: would require bidirectional coupling to mission planning.

Moderate fit. Safety Services can validate constraints and alert on violations, but does not own mission context intake, normalization, or performance mapping. Partial containment: constraint validation is a Safety Services concern, but context-to-performance mapping is broader than safety alone.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of mission context intake and normalization because it operates at the solution architecture level, above individual platforms (Vehicle, Teleoperation) and cross-cutting services (Safety Services). Mission context modeling, rule-based mapping, and traceability are solution-level concerns that must be decoupled from platform-specific execution logic. The DP is fully contained within NGHE's responsibility boundary with minimal external diffusion via well-defined Tasking (input) and Telemetry (output) interfaces.
containment_risks: Risk: If context normalization rules become highly domain-specific or vehicle-dependent, diffusion may increase as Vehicle or Teleoperation modules require custom mappings. Mitigation: enforce parameterization of context-to-performance rules to remain vehicle-agnostic.

Risk: Safety constraint validation may require tight coupling to Safety Services, creating bidirectional dependency. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert interface as a one-way subscription model where NGHE queries Safety Services for constraint definitions during intake.

Risk: Traceability documentation may grow large and require distributed storage or caching across modules. Mitigation: establish Telemetry as the authoritative interface for traceability export; other modules consume read-only copies.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete pipeline: structured intake of worksite mission context → normalization into a context model → application of context-to-performance mapping rules → validation against constraints → traceability documentation. The DP is sufficiently detailed and logically sound to satisfy the requirement that performance requirements be derived from worksite mission context.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Performance targets must flow to Vehicle and Teleoperation via Telemetry interface. If downstream modules require custom context-to-performance mappings specific to their operational modes, diffusion will increase. Mitigation: enforce parameterization of mapping rules to remain vehicle-agnostic and platform-independent.

Moderate diffusion: Safety constraint validation couples NGHE Solution to Safety Services via SafetyAlert (inout interface). If Safety Services constraint definitions change frequently or require real-time negotiation, bidirectional dependency may create tight coupling. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a one-way subscription model where NGHE queries Safety Services for constraint definitions during intake and caches them; Safety Services alerts are consumed asynchronously.

Potential diffusion: Traceability documentation may grow large and require distributed storage or caching. If multiple downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) need independent access to traceability records, logic for maintaining consistency may diffuse. Mitigation: establish Telemetry as the authoritative interface for traceability export; other modules consume read-only copies via event subscription or pull-based queries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of DP1 with a containment score of 0.92 and diffusion score of 0.08. Mission context intake, normalization, and context-to-performance mapping are solution-level orchestration concerns that must remain decoupled from platform-specific execution logic (Vehicle, Teleoperation) and cross-cutting services (Safety Services). The three external interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry out, SafetyAlert inout) are well-defined and minimize internal diffusion. The IM boundary is clear and responsibility allocation is appropriate.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criteria require: (1) a documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs, and (2) a history of baselines/calibration updates. Both are directly producible by DP1's traceability documentation and normalization pipeline. Verification can be performed by auditing the Telemetry interface output (performance targets + traceability records) against Tasking inputs and confirming that all performance targets have documented derivation paths. Baseline/calibration history can be maintained as a time-series record within the NGHE Solution module and exported via Telemetry.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. The FR is allocated to a single, well-scoped IM (NGHE Solution) with clear input/output interfaces. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are consumed as constraints during the intake phase (via Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces). The allocation is unambiguous: NGHE Solution owns the entire context-to-performance derivation pipeline. No competing ownership claims or unclear responsibility boundaries are evident.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define the parameterization strategy for context-to-performance mapping rules to ensure they remain vehicle-agnostic and reusable across different worksite mission contexts. Document rule schema and validation logic as part of the NGHE Solution design specification.

Clarify the SafetyAlert interface as a one-way subscription model: NGHE Solution queries Safety Services for constraint definitions during intake (pull-based); Safety Services publishes constraint violations asynchronously (push-based). Avoid bidirectional synchronous coupling.

Establish a traceability data model that includes: (1) worksite input parameters, (2) normalized context model state, (3) applied mapping rules and their parameters, (4) derived performance targets, (5) constraint validation results, and (6) timestamp/version information. Export this model via Telemetry as the authoritative source of truth.

Define acceptance criteria for baseline/calibration history: specify retention period, update frequency, and rollback procedures. Document how historical baselines are versioned and linked to specific worksite mission contexts.

Add a verification checkpoint: before exporting performance targets via Telemetry, validate that every target has a complete traceability chain back to at least one worksite input. Flag incomplete chains as errors and prevent export until resolved.

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gates all pass with high confidence (FR_DP: logical soundness, DP_IM: clear ownership with 0.92 containment, FR_IM: high verification feasibility). However, the recommended revisions reveal that DP1 (context-to-performance pipeline) contains multiple sub-concerns that require explicit decomposition to manage safety criticality and architectural clarity. Specifically: (1) parameterization strategy for mapping rules must be isolated as a sub-FR to ensure vehicle-agnostic reusability; (2) SafetyAlert interface coupling requires explicit sub-FR for constraint negotiation logic; (3) traceability data model and validation are non-trivial sub-concerns that warrant their own FR-DP-IM analysis; (4) baseline/calibration history management is a distinct operational concern. Given high safety criticality, these sub-concerns must be explicitly derived, designed, and verified rather than left implicit in DP1. Proceeding to next-level decomposition ensures that safety-critical constraint validation and traceability are not buried in orchestration logic.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Normalize worksite mission context (intake, parsing, schema validation) into a canonical context model that is independent of vehicle platform and operational mode.

FR1.2: Apply context-to-performance mapping rules in a parameterized, vehicle-agnostic manner such that rule sets can be reused across different worksite mission contexts without modification to core mapping logic.
FR1.3: Validate derived performance targets against worksite safety constraints and operational policies, with explicit traceability of constraint sources and validation results.
FR1.4: Maintain and export a complete traceability chain from worksite inputs through normalized context, applied rules, and derived targets, with versioning and audit trail support.
FR1.5: Manage baseline performance targets and calibration history with version control, rollback capability, and linkage to specific worksite mission contexts.

revise.why: DP1 is logically sound but operationally underspecified. The recommended revisions (parameterization strategy, SafetyAlert subscription model, traceability data model, baseline versioning, verification checkpoint) should be incorporated into the DP specification before detailed design. Specifically: (1) define the parameterization schema for context-to-performance rules as a first-class design concern; (2) explicitly model SafetyAlert as a pull-based intake + asynchronous alert subscription pattern; (3) formalize the traceability data model with required fields and validation rules; (4) specify baseline/calibration history as a versioned, queryable artifact with rollback semantics. These revisions elevate DP1 from a high-level pipeline description to an architecturally sound specification that can be safely decomposed and implemented.

assumptions: Worksite mission context is provided via Tasking interface in a structured, parseable format (schema TBD at next level).
Context-to-performance mapping rules are parameterizable and do not require vehicle-specific customization at the rule definition level.
Safety constraints are stable during a single mission execution cycle but may change between missions; caching during intake is acceptable.
Traceability records are immutable once created; updates are versioned appends, not in-place modifications.
Baseline/calibration history is retained for audit and rollback purposes; retention period and rollback procedures are defined by operational policy (TBD).
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational capacity to maintain traceability records and baseline history without external storage (or external storage is transparent via Telemetry interface).
open_risks: Safety criticality: If context-to-performance mapping rules are incorrect or incomplete, derived performance targets may violate safety constraints. Mitigation: FR1.3 (constraint validation) must include a verification checkpoint that flags incomplete traceability chains before export. Acceptance criteria must require 100% traceability coverage.

Diffusion risk: If downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation) require custom context-to-performance mappings, parameterization strategy (FR1.2) may fail. Mitigation: enforce strict separation between vehicle-agnostic rule parameters and vehicle-specific execution logic; any vehicle-specific customization must be pushed to Vehicle module, not NGHE Solution.

Coupling risk: SafetyAlert interface (FR1.3) may create tight coupling if Safety Services constraint definitions change frequently. Mitigation: implement pull-based intake with caching (as recommended); define constraint versioning and change notification protocol.

Scalability risk: Traceability records (FR1.4) may grow large if mission contexts are complex or execution time is long. Mitigation: define data retention and archival policies; establish Telemetry as the authoritative export interface to avoid duplication.

Verification risk: Baseline/calibration history (FR1.5) requires clear acceptance criteria for rollback procedures and version linkage. Mitigation: define rollback semantics explicitly (e.g., point-in-time restore, linked to mission context version) and test rollback procedures as part of acceptance testing.
evidence_needed: Worksite mission context schema: document the structure, data types, and validation rules for inputs provided via Tasking interface. Include examples from representative worksite scenarios.

Context-to-performance mapping rule schema: define the parameterization strategy, rule syntax, and validation logic. Demonstrate that rules can be reused across at least two distinct worksite mission contexts without modification.
Safety constraint model: document the structure of safety constraints, their sources (worksite safety plan, operational policies), and how they are versioned and cached during intake.

Traceability data model: provide a formal schema (e.g., JSON, protobuf) for traceability records, including all required fields (worksite inputs, context model state, applied rules, derived targets, constraint validation results, timestamps, versions). Include examples.

Baseline/calibration history specification: define retention period, update frequency, versioning scheme, and rollback procedures. Document how baselines are linked to worksite mission contexts and how historical baselines are queried.
Verification checkpoint specification: define the logic for validating traceability chains, including what constitutes a 'complete' chain and how incomplete chains are flagged and reported.
Interface specifications: formalize Tasking (input), Telemetry (output), and SafetyAlert (inout) interfaces with message schemas, timing constraints, and error handling procedures.
